title: finding-record.v1 — a finding the self-monitoring system has taken ownership of
description: The shape the engine's self-monitoring stores for a finding it is tracking. It builds on the shared finding.v1 base (a severity, a message, a location) and adds the three things the self-monitoring needs to act on it: which signal it came from (so repeats of the same signal collapse onto one tracked item instead of piling up), and when that signal was first and most-recently seen (so a tracked item can be closed once its signal has stayed away). The severity here is narrowed to the self-monitoring's own two grades — distinct from the grades a check or a review uses. This file is reference data only: nothing is validated against it at commit time yet; its own correctness as a schema is checked automatically.

severity.description: How the self-monitoring grades this finding — one of exactly two. 'trust-critical' means a safety check or gate that could not run at all: it is tracked the moment it is seen, because the engine may be flying blind. 'persistent-but-benign' means a low-impact signal that keeps recurring: it is tracked only after it has shown up enough times to be worth your attention, so a one-off blip never bothers you. These two grades are internal and never shown to a person as-is; anything you see is translated into plain language first. The open severity of the finding.v1 base is narrowed to these two here, on purpose.

message.description: A clear, complete, plain-language statement of what the self-monitoring noticed — what it is, why it matters, and what (if anything) you must decide — written so both a person and the engine can act on it, with no backstage vocabulary. This is the prose that becomes a tracked item's body, so it carries the plain-language contract: it names the engine noticing something about its OWN health, never a problem with your product.

location.description: Where the finding is, or null when it is not tied to a file. This is per-occurrence detail that enriches the message only — it is NEVER part of how repeats are collapsed (that is the source below). When present it points to a file and, optionally, a line.

file.description: Path of the file the finding concerns, relative to the repository root.

line.description: Line number within the file (counting from 1), or null when the finding is not tied to a specific line.

source_id.description: The stable identity of the SIGNAL the finding came from — the id of the rule, surface, or stream that emitted it — and nothing tied to a single occurrence (never the file, run, or value it happened to be seen on). This is how repeats are collapsed: every recurrence of the same signal lands on the one tracked item rather than opening a new one. Because it is tied to the signal's source and the self-monitoring only ever watches the engine's own surfaces, the number of tracked items can only grow with the engine's own list of signals — never with the size of your product or how often a signal recurs.

first_seen.description: When this signal was first seen in its current run of appearances, written as a UTC moment ending in Z (for example 2026-06-02T14:30:00Z). Used to tell how long a recurring signal has persisted; it starts over if the signal goes away and later returns. The trailing-Z UTC shape is what is actually enforced (the date-time format is only a hint).

last_seen.description: When this signal was most recently seen, written as a UTC moment ending in Z. The self-monitoring reads how long it has been since this — counted in observations, not wall-clock — to decide when a now-quiet tracked item should be closed automatically. The trailing-Z UTC shape is what is actually enforced (the date-time format is only a hint).
